source_paper_title: On Averaging and Extrapolation for Gradient Descent
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Luner_2025, title={On Averaging and Extrapolation for Gradient Descent}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0449}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0449}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Luner, Alan and Grimmer, Benjamin}, year={2025}, month=july }
context:
Section 3.2.1 (Discussion of Conditions (C1) and (C2)), page 12.

That is, numerical results suggest that the converses of Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 are true, i.e., (C1)/(C2) hold if and only if averaging is not beneficial. We state this formally in the following conjecture.

Conjecture 3.3. Consider gradient descent with stepsizes \(h = (h_0, \dots, h_{N-1}) > 0\). For any \(L, D > 0\), if the worst-case final iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in \mathcal{F}_{L,D}} f\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\Bigr) - \inf f < \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.\]
Similarly, if the worst-case final iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in \mathcal{F}_{L,D}} \|\nabla f\bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\bigr)\| < \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.\]
title: Prove tight worst-case characterization of gradient descent averaging benefit conditions
background_latex:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}\) be convex with \(L\)-Lipschitz continuous gradient (\(L\)-smooth). Let \(x^\star\in\arg\min f\) exist, and assume an initial point \(x_0\) satisfies \(\|x_0-x^\star\|\le D\). Consider (unconstrained) gradient descent with a fixed, predetermined stepsize sequence \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})\) with \(h_k>0\):
\[
 x_{k+1}=x_k-\frac{h_k}{L}\nabla f(x_k),\qquad k=0,\dots,N-1.
\]
(So the iterates \(x_0,\dots,x_N\) are generated by this recursion.)

An averaging scheme is a weight vector \(\sigma=(\sigma_0,\dots,\sigma_N)\in\Delta_{N+1}\), where the simplex is
\[
\Delta_{N+1}:=\{\sigma\in\mathbb{R}^{N+1}_+ : \sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j=1\}.
\]
The reported point under averaging is
\[
 x_\sigma:=\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j.
\]
The degenerate averaging scheme that returns the last iterate is \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}:=(0,\dots,0,1)\), for which \(x_{\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}}=x_N\). An averaging scheme is called nondegenerate if \(x_\sigma\ne x_N\) (equivalently, \(\sigma\ne\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\)).

Define the worst-case last-iterate bounds
\[
\text{(C1)}\quad \max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_N)-f(x^\star)\bigr) = \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2},
\]
\[
\text{(C2)}\quad \max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_N)\| = \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.
\]
(These equalities assert that the displayed expressions exactly characterize the worst-case objective gap and worst-case gradient norm at the last iterate for the given stepsizes.)

The paper proves one direction: if (C1) holds, then \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\) uniquely minimizes the worst-case objective gap over all \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\); similarly, if (C2) holds, then \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\) uniquely minimizes the worst-case gradient norm over all \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\). It then proposes a converse statement as a conjecture.
problem_definition_latex:
Establish (or refute) the following converse characterizations for gradient descent with fixed stepsizes \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})>0\):

1) Objective-gap converse: If the worst-case last-iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), i.e.,
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_N)-f(x^\star)\bigr) \ne \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2},
\]
then there exists a nondegenerate averaging scheme \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_\sigma)-f(x^\star)\bigr) 
< \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.
\]

2) Gradient-norm converse: If the worst-case last-iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), i.e.,
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_N)\| \ne \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1},
\]
then there exists a nondegenerate averaging scheme \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_\sigma)\| 
< \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.
\]

Equivalently, prove that (C1) holds if and only if averaging cannot improve the worst-case objective-gap guarantee over returning \(x_N\), and (C2) holds if and only if averaging cannot improve the worst-case gradient-norm guarantee over returning \(x_N\).
latex:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}\) be convex with \(L\)-Lipschitz continuous gradient (\(L\)-smooth). Let \(x^\star\in\arg\min f\) exist, and assume an initial point \(x_0\) satisfies \(\|x_0-x^\star\|\le D\). Consider (unconstrained) gradient descent with a fixed, predetermined stepsize sequence \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})\) with \(h_k>0\):
\[
 x_{k+1}=x_k-\frac{h_k}{L}\nabla f(x_k),\qquad k=0,\dots,N-1.
\]
(So the iterates \(x_0,\dots,x_N\) are generated by this recursion.)

An averaging scheme is a weight vector \(\sigma=(\sigma_0,\dots,\sigma_N)\in\Delta_{N+1}\), where the simplex is
\[
\Delta_{N+1}:=\{\sigma\in\mathbb{R}^{N+1}_+ : \sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j=1\}.
\]
The reported point under averaging is
\[
 x_\sigma:=\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j.
\]
The degenerate averaging scheme that returns the last iterate is \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}:=(0,\dots,0,1)\), for which \(x_{\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}}=x_N\). An averaging scheme is called nondegenerate if \(x_\sigma\ne x_N\) (equivalently, \(\sigma\ne\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\)).

Define the worst-case last-iterate bounds
\[
\text{(C1)}\quad \max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_N)-f(x^\star)\bigr) = \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2},
\]
\[
\text{(C2)}\quad \max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_N)\| = \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.
\]
(These equalities assert that the displayed expressions exactly characterize the worst-case objective gap and worst-case gradient norm at the last iterate for the given stepsizes.)

The paper proves one direction: if (C1) holds, then \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\) uniquely minimizes the worst-case objective gap over all \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\); similarly, if (C2) holds, then \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\) uniquely minimizes the worst-case gradient norm over all \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\). It then proposes a converse statement as a conjecture.

Establish (or refute) the following converse characterizations for gradient descent with fixed stepsizes \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})>0\):

1) Objective-gap converse: If the worst-case last-iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), i.e.,
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_N)-f(x^\star)\bigr) \ne \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2},
\]
then there exists a nondegenerate averaging scheme \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \bigl(f(x_\sigma)-f(x^\star)\bigr) 
< \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.
\]

2) Gradient-norm converse: If the worst-case last-iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), i.e.,
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_N)\| \ne \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1},
\]
then there exists a nondegenerate averaging scheme \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[
\max_{(x_0,f):\ \|x_0-x^\star\|\le D} \|\nabla f(x_\sigma)\| 
< \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.
\]

Equivalently, prove that (C1) holds if and only if averaging cannot improve the worst-case objective-gap guarantee over returning \(x_N\), and (C2) holds if and only if averaging cannot improve the worst-case gradient-norm guarantee over returning \(x_N\).
open_problem_quote:
That is, numerical results suggest that the converses of Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 are true, i.e., (C1)/(C2) hold if and only if averaging is not beneficial. We state this formally in the following conjecture.

Conjecture 3.3. Consider gradient descent with stepsizes \(h = (h_0, \dots, h_{N-1}) > 0\). For any \(L, D > 0\), if the worst-case final iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in \mathcal{F}_{L,D}} f\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\Bigr) - \inf f < \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.\]
Similarly, if the worst-case final iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in \mathcal{F}_{L,D}} \|\nabla f\bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\bigr)\| < \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.\]
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The conjecture can be tested for fixed small \(N\) and stepsize sequences by solving the associated PEP semidefinite programs for both the last iterate and the best averaged iterate, and checking whether an improving nondegenerate \(\sigma\) exists whenever (C1)/(C2) fail. This makes automated search over stepsize patterns and averaging weights feasible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects (stepsize sequences \(h\) and averaging weights \(\sigma\)) admit a direct computational fitness signal via PEP SDPs approximating the worst-case gaps/gradient norms. An evolutionary loop could search for counterexamples (instances where (C1)/(C2) fail but averaging still does not help, or vice versa) or discover structural patterns suggesting a proof strategy.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The conjectured converse (Conjecture 3.3 in Luner--Grimmer) asks for a tight equivalence between two phenomena for fixed-step gradient descent on the smooth convex class \(\mathcal F_{L,D}\): (i) the last-iterate worst-case objective gap and gradient norm are exactly the Huber-attained expressions (C1)/(C2), and (ii) no nondegenerate averaging \(x_\sigma\) can strictly improve the corresponding minimax guarantee. Luner--Grimmer prove the forward direction: if (C1) (resp. (C2)) holds, then \(\sigma^{\mathrm{last}}\) uniquely minimizes the worst-case objective gap (resp. gradient norm) over all \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\), using the fact that the Huber function is affine over the convex hull of iterates and makes any nontrivial convex combination strictly worse.

Forward-citing work to date does not resolve the converse in general, but it expands the catalog of stepsize schedules for which (C1)/(C2)-type equalities are now known to hold tightly, thereby strengthening the empirical alignment observed in Luner--Grimmer’s Figure 1. In particular, "Relaxed proximal point algorithm: Tight complexity bounds and acceleration without momentum" proves tight last-iterate bounds for gradient descent under the silver stepsize schedule, establishing the exact (C1)/(C2) forms (depending only on \(\sum h_i\)) for that family. "Accelerated objective gap and gradient norm convergence for gradient descent via long steps" similarly provides tight last-iterate guarantees for certain recursively defined long-step schedules and exhibits explicit extremal quadratic/Huber-type instances; it also notes that averaging can be strictly worse for those schedules, consistent with the ‘no averaging benefit’ side when last-iterate bounds are tight. "Composing optimized stepsize schedules for gradient descent" develops compositional machinery to build stepsize schedules with certifiable tight last-iterate guarantees, offering a pathway to systematically generate further instances where (C1)/(C2) hold.

The main gap remains: a general proof (or counterexample) that whenever the last-iterate worst-case deviates from (C1) or (C2), there must exist a nondegenerate \(\sigma\) with strictly smaller minimax objective gap or gradient norm than the Huber-based expressions. Promising directions suggested by the existing PEP-based framework include: (a) analyzing the joint minimax problem \(\min_{\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}}\max_{f\in\mathcal F_{L,D}}\) via SDP duality to relate optimal \(\sigma\) to slackness of the last-iterate dual certificate; and (b) leveraging the composability/inductive constructions for stepsizes to reduce the converse to verifying a finite set of extremal patterns. At present, however, no forward citation provides a full converse characterization, so the problem remains open.

sources.title: Relaxed proximal point algorithm: Tight complexity bounds and acceleration without momentum
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves tight worst-case convergence bounds for the relaxed proximal point algorithm (RPPA), and as a byproduct proves [LG24, Conjecture 3.2] for gradient descent (GD) with the silver stepsize schedule \(\pi^{(m)}\) (with \(N=2^m-1\)): for any convex \(L\)-smooth \(h\), \[h(x_N)-h(x^\star)\le \frac{L\|x_0-x^\star\|^2}{4\rho^m-2}=\frac{L\|x_0-x^\star\|^2}{2(2\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\alpha_i+1)},\qquad \|\nabla h(x_N)\|\le \frac{L\|x_0-x^\star\|}{\rho^m}=\frac{L\|x_0-x^\star\|}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\alpha_i+1},\] and both bounds are tight (Theorem 5.2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a converse characterization: for fixed GD stepsizes \(h\), the last iterate is worst-case optimal among all averages iff the last-iterate worst-case equals the explicit formulas (C1)/(C2). This paper does not study averaging schemes \(x_\sigma\) at all, nor does it prove any ‘if and only if’ link between tight last-iterate bounds and impossibility of improvement by averaging. However, it is relevant because it establishes additional instances where the last-iterate worst-case bounds take the exact (C1)/(C2)-type form (depending only on \(\sum h_i\)), notably proving tightness for the silver stepsize schedule conjectured in [LG24]. These results support the ‘only if’ direction’s plausibility by expanding the class of stepsizes for which (C1)/(C2)-style equalities hold, but they do not address the converse implication about existence of improving nondegenerate averages when (C1)/(C2) fail.

sources.title: Accelerated objective gap and gradient norm convergence for gradient descent via long steps
sources.theorem_or_result:
For specific long-stepsize schedules \,\(h^{(k)}_{\mathrm{left}}\) and \,\(h^{(k)}_{\mathrm{right}}\) (and their concatenation), the paper proves tight last-iterate worst-case guarantees for L-smooth convex minimization after \,\(N=2^k-1\) (or \,\(2^{k+1}-2\)) steps:
- (Gradient-norm from function gap) With \,\(h=h^{(k)}_{\mathrm{left}}\): \(\tfrac12\|\nabla f(x_N)\|^2 \le \tfrac{L(f(x_0)-f(x^\star))}{r_k}\).
- (Objective gap from distance) With \,\(h=h^{(k)}_{\mathrm{right}}\): \(f(x_N)-f(x^\star) \le \tfrac{L}{2}\,\tfrac{\|x_0-x^\star\|^2}{r_k}\).
Here \(r_k = 2\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1 = \prod_{i=0}^{N-1}(1-h_i)^{-2}\) and \(r_k \asymp N^{\log_2(\varrho)}\) with \(\varrho=1+\sqrt2\). The bounds are claimed tight (attained) on a quadratic and on a Huber-type function.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a converse characterization: if the last-iterate worst-case is not exactly the (C1)/(C2) expressions \(LD^2/(4\sum h_i+2)\) and \(LD/(\sum h_i+1)\), then some nontrivial averaging \(x_\sigma\) should strictly improve the worst-case guarantee. This paper does not prove that converse in general. However, it provides evidence in a particular direction for certain long-stepsize schedules: it constructs explicit worst-case functions (quadratic and Huber) that attain the paper’s last-iterate bounds, and it states (Remark 2) that for these stepsizes, reporting an average \(\sum \sigma_i x_i\) instead of \(x_N\) strictly worsens worst-case performance. That is consistent with the ‘no averaging benefit’ side of the conjectured equivalence, but only for these specific schedules and without establishing the (C1)/(C2) exact worst-case characterizations for arbitrary \(h\) or the full converse implication.

sources.title: Composing optimized stepsize schedules for gradient descent
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a theory of “composable” fixed stepsize schedules for gradient descent on smooth convex functions. In particular, it defines f-composable schedules (tight final objective-gap guarantee) and g-composable schedules (tight final gradient-norm guarantee) characterized by the equalities \(\eta=\frac{1}{1+2\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}h_i}=\prod_{i=0}^{n-1}(h_i-1)^2\), and proves inductive composition theorems (Theorems 1–3) showing that certain join operations on schedules preserve f-/g-/s-composability with explicitly computable new rates. It then constructs optimized basic schedules (OBS-F/OBS-G) conjectured minimax-optimal for minimizing \(f(x_n)-f(x^\star)\) or \(\|\nabla f(x_n)\|\) at the last iterate.
sources.connection: The open problem concerns when averaging \(x_\sigma=\sum_j\sigma_j x_j\) can strictly improve the worst-case guarantee over the last iterate \(x_N\), and conjectures an equivalence with whether the last-iterate worst-case equals the closed forms (C1)/(C2). This citing paper does not study averaging over iterates at all; it studies how to choose fixed stepsizes \(h\) to optimize last-iterate performance and gives tight certificates/worst-case instances (quadratic/Huber) for certain structured schedules. Indirectly, it provides tools to identify stepsize schedules for which last-iterate bounds are tight and witnessed by specific extremal functions, which is adjacent to verifying conditions like (C1)/(C2), but it does not address the converse ‘if not (C1)/(C2) then averaging helps’ characterization.
